control.prompt_text: A project has two funding options. Option A guarantees a return of $80,000. Option B has a 40% chance of returning $250,000 and a 60% chance of returning $0. Which option has the higher expected monetary value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Option A — guaranteed $80,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Option B — 40% chance of $250,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both options have equal expected value

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cannot be determined from the information given

explicit.prompt_text: Maya says: 'I'd rather lock in a gain of $400 than take a 50-50 shot at $900, even though the gamble is worth more. Losing feels so much worse.' Which response best identifies the error in Maya's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maya is being rational — certainty always has inherent value

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maya is letting loss aversion override expected-value reasoning; the gamble's EV ($450) exceeds the certain $400

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maya's preference is valid because the gamble could result in a loss of opportunity

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no error — both choices reflect reasonable personal risk tolerance

version_a.prompt_text:
Meridian Consulting is reviewing its flexible-work policy for the customer-success division. The HR team has collected six months of data showing that the revised scheduling arrangement has reduced coordinator burnout significantly. The division runs 38 people across four regional clusters. Internal satisfaction surveys were completed last quarter. The new policy has added $120,000 to annual payroll savings through reduced overtime and lower turnover replacement costs.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term team morale prospects for this division under its current trajectory?

version_b.prompt_text:
Meridian Consulting is reviewing its flexible-work policy for the customer-success division. The HR team has collected six months of data showing that the revised scheduling arrangement has reduced coordinator burnout significantly. The division runs 38 people across four regional clusters. Internal satisfaction surveys were completed last quarter. Without the new policy, the division would continue to lose $120,000 annually to overtime overruns and turnover replacement costs.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term team morale prospects for this division under its current trajectory?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a presents the scheduling policy outcome as a $120K annual savings (gain); version_b presents the identical outcome as an ongoing $120K annual loss avoided. The question asks about team morale, not the financial figure.

control.prompt_text: A safety intervention costs $500,000. Without it, there is a 1-in-4 chance of an incident costing $3,000,000. With the intervention the probability of the incident drops to zero. Should the firm adopt the intervention based purely on expected cost minimization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — expected cost of no intervention is $750,000, which exceeds the $500,000 cost

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — the intervention is more expensive than the guaranteed outcome

choices.label: C
choices.text: It depends on the firm's risk tolerance, not expected value alone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both choices have the same expected cost

explicit.prompt_text: Carlos refuses to cancel a failing project because his team has already spent $2,000,000 on it, even though continuing will lose another $800,000 with no expected benefit. He says 'We can't let that investment go to waste.' What is the rational course of action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue — the sunk costs justify seeing the project through

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cancel — sunk costs are irrelevant; only future costs and benefits should guide the decision

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue — momentum and team morale justify further investment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seek additional funding before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Brightfield Manufacturing operates a precision-components line serving three automotive OEMs. The plant recently completed a six-month trial of a revised quality-control protocol. Process engineers reviewed throughput data and supplier feedback. The plant employs 210 floor staff across two shifts. Since introducing the new protocol, the defect rate on the primary assembly line has improved by 15 percentage points.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the operational efficiency of this manufacturing line?

version_b.prompt_text:
Brightfield Manufacturing operates a precision-components line serving three automotive OEMs. The plant recently completed a six-month trial of a revised quality-control protocol. Process engineers reviewed throughput data and supplier feedback. The plant employs 210 floor staff across two shifts. Without the new protocol, the defect rate on the primary assembly line would remain 15 percentage points higher than current levels.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the operational efficiency of this manufacturing line?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the QC protocol as a 15-point improvement in defect rate (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 15-point higher defect rate (loss). The question asks about operational efficiency.

control.prompt_text: Option A: receive $200 with certainty. Option B: flip a fair coin — heads pays $500, tails pays $0. Which has the higher expected value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Option A — expected value $200

choices.label: B
choices.text: Option B — expected value $250

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are equal in expected value

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expected value cannot be calculated here

explicit.prompt_text: A procurement manager states: 'I'll pay a $10,000 premium to avoid any chance of a $15,000 penalty, even though the penalty has only a 50% chance of occurring.' Evaluate this decision from an expected-value standpoint.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The premium is justified — avoiding risk is always prudent in procurement

choices.label: B
choices.text: The premium is irrational; the expected penalty ($7,500) is less than the certain premium ($10,000)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The decision is reasonable given contractual obligations

choices.label: D
choices.text: More information is needed before evaluating the decision

version_a.prompt_text:
Northgate Financial Services is conducting its annual review of its branch-network consolidation plan. The CFO's office prepared a cost analysis covering 14 regional branches. The consolidation has been ongoing for 18 months and involves staff reassignments across three metropolitan clusters. The initiative generates $2,400,000 in quarterly overhead savings by merging back-office functions.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term organizational sustainability of Northgate's current operating model?

version_b.prompt_text:
Northgate Financial Services is conducting its annual review of its branch-network consolidation plan. The CFO's office prepared a cost analysis covering 14 regional branches. The consolidation has been ongoing for 18 months and involves staff reassignments across three metropolitan clusters. Without this consolidation, the organization would continue losing $2,400,000 each quarter to redundant back-office overhead.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term organizational sustainability of Northgate's current operating model?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the consolidation as $2.4M quarterly savings (gain); version_b frames it as avoiding a $2.4M quarterly loss. The question asks about long-term organizational sustainability.

control.prompt_text: A clinical trial compares two treatments for a rare condition affecting 1,200 patients. Treatment X saves exactly 400 patients. Treatment Y has a 1/3 chance of saving all 1,200 patients and a 2/3 chance of saving none. Which treatment saves more patients in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Treatment X — saves 400 with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Treatment Y — expected savings of 400 patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both save the same number in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treatment Y saves more in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: An investor says: 'I won't sell my losing stock even though buying a different stock has higher expected return. Selling would mean admitting a loss.' What cognitive bias is driving this behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overconfidence bias — the investor believes the stock will recover

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss aversion combined with the disposition effect — the pain of realizing a loss distorts the optimal sell decision

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anchoring bias — the investor is anchored to the purchase price

choices.label: D
choices.text: This is rational patience, not a bias

version_a.prompt_text:
Cascade Software's enterprise sales team introduced an automated renewal workflow for mid-market accounts 18 months ago. The customer-success department conducted a cohort analysis across 340 accounts. The team has 27 account managers distributed across four territories. The new workflow has increased year-over-year customer retention by 22 percentage points.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate stakeholder confidence in the enterprise sales team's current strategic direction?

version_b.prompt_text:
Cascade Software's enterprise sales team introduced an automated renewal workflow for mid-market accounts 18 months ago. The customer-success department conducted a cohort analysis across 340 accounts. The team has 27 account managers distributed across four territories. Without the new workflow, year-over-year customer retention would be 22 percentage points lower than it is today.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate stakeholder confidence in the enterprise sales team's current strategic direction?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the workflow as a 22-point retention gain; version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 22-point retention loss. The question asks about stakeholder confidence.

control.prompt_text: Two investment strategies are proposed. Strategy A yields a net gain of $15,000 with certainty. Strategy B yields a net gain of $60,000 with 40% probability and a net loss of $10,000 with 60% probability. Which strategy has the higher expected value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Strategy A — EV = $15,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Strategy B — EV = $18,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both strategies have the same expected value

choices.label: D
choices.text: Strategy A has higher expected value

explicit.prompt_text: A product team declines to launch a feature that has a 70% chance of increasing revenue by $500,000 and a 30% chance of generating a $100,000 refund cost. The team lead says: 'The downside feels too painful.' What does expected-value analysis recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Do not launch — any risk of refunds should be avoided

choices.label: B
choices.text: Launch — EV is positive at +$320,000, and the team lead's framing reflects loss aversion rather than rational analysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delay until more certainty about outcomes is available

choices.label: D
choices.text: The team lead's caution is appropriate given customer experience risks

version_a.prompt_text:
Pinebrook Technologies is evaluating a proposal to migrate its data analytics platform to a managed cloud service. The infrastructure team prepared a two-year total-cost-of-ownership model covering compute, storage, and support. The engineering department has 45 staff, including eight dedicated to platform operations. The migration is projected to save $850,000 annually in infrastructure and maintenance costs.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the implementation feasibility of this migration project?

version_b.prompt_text:
Pinebrook Technologies is evaluating a proposal to migrate its data analytics platform to a managed cloud service. The infrastructure team prepared a two-year total-cost-of-ownership model covering compute, storage, and storage support. The engineering department has 45 staff, including eight dedicated to platform operations. Without the migration, the company would continue losing $850,000 annually to avoidable infrastructure and maintenance overhead.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the implementation feasibility of this migration project?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the migration as an $850K annual savings (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding an $850K annual infrastructure loss. The question asks about implementation feasibility.

control.prompt_text: An engineering team must choose between two quality-control plans. Plan P eliminates 300 defective units per production run for certain. Plan Q eliminates all 900 defective units with probability 0.25 and eliminates none with probability 0.75. Which plan removes more defects per run in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plan P — removes 300 units with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Plan Q — expected removal of 225 units

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both plans remove the same number in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan P removes more defects per run in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: HR director Priya says: 'I'd rather keep a known under-performer on staff than risk hiring an external candidate who might not work out, even though the external hire has a 75% chance of being far more productive.' Which statement best describes the flaw in Priya's reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Priya is correct — retaining existing staff reduces transition costs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Priya is weighting the potential loss of a bad hire more heavily than the expected gain from a better hire, a classic loss-aversion error

choices.label: C
choices.text: Priya's preference reflects legitimate risk management, not bias

choices.label: D
choices.text: Priya should gather more data before making a personnel decision

version_a.prompt_text:
Vantage Insurance Group's compliance division completed a regulatory readiness review covering its claims-processing unit. The review spanned 12 weeks and involved 23 process audits across the underwriting and settlement teams. The division employs 160 staff across six functional groups. The new compliance framework has prevented $500,000 in regulatory fines that were assessed against peer firms for similar infractions.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the risk management maturity of Vantage's compliance division?

version_b.prompt_text:
Vantage Insurance Group's compliance division completed a regulatory readiness review covering its claims-processing unit. The review spanned 12 weeks and involved 23 process audits across the underwriting and settlement teams. The division employs 160 staff across six functional groups. Without the new compliance framework, the division would face $500,000 in regulatory fines, matching penalties assessed against peer firms for similar infractions.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the risk management maturity of Vantage's compliance division?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the compliance framework as preventing $500K in fines (gain framed as avoided loss — but presented positively as 'prevented'); version_b frames the same situation as facing $500K in fines without the framework (explicit loss). The question asks about risk management maturity.

control.prompt_text: A hedge fund manager considers two positions. Position A earns $1,200,000 with certainty. Position B earns $4,000,000 with 25% probability and $400,000 with 75% probability. Which position has the higher expected return?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Position A — EV = $1,200,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Position B — EV = $1,300,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Position A — it is larger than B's EV

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both positions have equal expected return

explicit.prompt_text: A CFO declines a hedging contract that would cap the firm's losses at $1,000,000 but costs $600,000, even though without the hedge there is a 55% chance of losing $1,000,000. The CFO says 'We shouldn't throw away $600,000.' Is the CFO's reasoning sound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — spending $600,000 to hedge is wasteful when the company might not face a loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — the expected loss without the hedge ($550,000) is less than the hedge cost ($600,000), so the CFO's intuition is actually correct on EV grounds

choices.label: C
choices.text: No — hedging is always rational regardless of expected value

choices.label: D
choices.text: The CFO should accept the hedge to protect against worst-case outcomes

version_a.prompt_text:
Helio Labs launched a redesigned onboarding flow for its project-management SaaS tool eight months ago. The product team analyzed activation data from 4,200 new accounts across three pricing tiers. The company has 18 product staff organized into two squads. The redesigned onboarding has increased feature adoption within the first 30 days of signup by 30 percentage points.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the product-market fit of Helio Labs' current offering?

version_b.prompt_text:
Helio Labs launched a redesigned onboarding flow for its project-management SaaS tool eight months ago. The product team analyzed activation data from 4,200 new accounts across three pricing tiers. The company has 18 product staff organized into two squads. Without the redesigned onboarding, feature adoption in the first 30 days of signup would be 30 percentage points lower than it is today.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the product-market fit of Helio Labs' current offering?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the onboarding redesign as a 30-point adoption increase (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as a 30-point adoption shortfall without the redesign (loss). The question asks about product-market fit.

control.prompt_text: A product recall costs $2,000,000. Without a recall, there is a 30% chance of a lawsuit costing $8,000,000 and a 70% chance of no lawsuit. What is the expected cost of not recalling?

explicit.prompt_text: A manager says: 'Framing a budget cut as saving $500K feels far less painful to present to the board than framing the same cut as avoiding a $500K loss, even though the financial outcome is identical.' What does this illustrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Effective communication strategy — framing matters for stakeholder buy-in

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain/loss framing bias — the psychological impact differs even though the objective outcome is the same, which can distort rational decision-making

choices.label: C
choices.text: This is a reasonable preference for positive messaging

choices.label: D
choices.text: The manager should present both framings and let the board decide

version_a.prompt_text:
Redwood Group's talent operations team deployed an AI-assisted performance-review tool across its 12 business units last year. The deployment covered 85 managers responsible for reviewing approximately 1,100 employees. HR analytics compared cycle times before and after deployment across two annual review periods. The tool saves managers an average of 40 hours per month previously spent on administrative review preparation.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the organizational efficiency of Redwood Group's talent management function?

version_b.prompt_text:
Redwood Group's talent operations team deployed an AI-assisted performance-review tool across its 12 business units last year. The deployment covered 85 managers responsible for reviewing approximately 1,100 employees. HR analytics compared cycle times before and after deployment across two annual review periods. Without the tool, managers would lose an average of 40 hours per month to administrative review preparation tasks.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the organizational efficiency of Redwood Group's talent management function?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the AI tool as saving 40 hours/month (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 40-hour/month loss of manager time. The question asks about organizational efficiency.

control.prompt_text: Option A costs $0 and has a 10% chance of incurring a $50,000 penalty. Option B costs $4,000 upfront and eliminates the penalty entirely. Which option minimizes expected cost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Option A — expected cost $5,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Option B — expected cost $4,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both options have the same expected cost

choices.label: D
choices.text: Option A minimizes expected cost

explicit.prompt_text: An operations director refuses a process change that has a net expected benefit of $200,000 per year. 'There's a 20% chance it makes things worse,' she says. 'That 20% downside keeps me up at night more than the 80% upside excites me.' Identify the normatively incorrect aspect of this reasoning.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her concern about the downside is rational operational risk management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Her asymmetric weighting of equal-probability outcomes reflects loss aversion, which leads her to reject a positive-EV change

choices.label: C
choices.text: She needs more information about the 20% downside scenario before deciding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rejecting low-probability downsides is standard corporate governance

version_a.prompt_text:
Crestwood Pharmaceuticals is reviewing its self-insured health benefits program for the fiscal year. The benefits administration team compiled utilization data across 620 enrolled employees in six plan categories. The finance team modeled premium equivalents against a fully-insured benchmark. The self-insured model saves the company $10,000 per employee annually compared to a fully-insured equivalent program.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term financial health of Crestwood's employee benefits strategy?

version_b.prompt_text:
Crestwood Pharmaceuticals is reviewing its self-insured health benefits program for the fiscal year. The benefits administration team compiled utilization data across 620 enrolled employees in six plan categories. The finance team modeled premium equivalents against a fully-insured benchmark. Switching away from the self-insured model would cost the company an additional $10,000 per employee annually relative to current spending.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the long-term financial health of Crestwood's employee benefits strategy?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the self-insured model as saving $10K per employee (gain); version_b frames abandoning it as costing an additional $10K per employee (loss). The question asks about long-term financial health.

control.prompt_text: A logistics manager compares two shipping contracts. Contract A guarantees delivery of 500 units per week. Contract B delivers 800 units per week with 60% probability and 100 units per week with 40% probability. Which contract delivers more units per week in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Contract A — 500 units with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contract B — expected 520 units per week

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both contracts deliver the same units in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Contract A delivers more units in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A trader says: 'I hold losing positions three times longer than winning ones because locking in a loss feels catastrophic, even when analysis says I should exit.' What is the rational correction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold losing positions until they recover — patience is rewarded

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exit positions based on future expected value, not on the emotional cost of realizing a past loss; loss aversion is distorting the decision

choices.label: C
choices.text: The trader's caution is appropriate given market volatility

choices.label: D
choices.text: Apply a fixed time rule to both winning and losing positions

version_a.prompt_text:
Holloway Logistics operates a network of 11 distribution centers across the upper Midwest. The sustainability team completed a two-year facilities audit covering lighting, HVAC, and refrigeration systems. The facilities management group has 34 staff dedicated to operations and maintenance. The energy retrofit program has reduced total energy consumption across the network by 18%.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the environmental sustainability of Holloway Logistics' operations?

version_b.prompt_text:
Holloway Logistics operates a network of 11 distribution centers across the upper Midwest. The sustainability team completed a two-year facilities audit covering lighting, HVAC, and refrigeration systems. The facilities management group has 34 staff dedicated to operations and maintenance. Without the energy retrofit program, total energy consumption across the network would be 18% higher than it is today.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the environmental sustainability of Holloway Logistics' operations?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the retrofit as an 18% reduction in energy consumption (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding an 18% higher consumption level (loss). The question asks about environmental sustainability.

control.prompt_text: A sales team evaluates two promotional tactics. Tactic A generates a guaranteed $90,000 in additional revenue. Tactic B generates $200,000 with 55% probability and $0 with 45% probability. Which tactic generates more expected revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tactic A — $90,000 certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tactic B — expected $110,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both tactics generate the same expected revenue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tactic A generates more expected revenue

explicit.prompt_text: A real estate developer says: 'Even though selling this property now yields the highest expected return, I won't sell below my purchase price — I'd rather wait years at a lower expected return than lock in a nominal loss.' What does normative decision theory recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Wait — the purchase price is a valid reference point for pricing decisions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sell — the purchase price is a sunk cost; the normative decision depends only on future expected returns, and the aversion to a nominal loss is irrational

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refinance the property instead to avoid the loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seek an independent appraisal before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Apex Digital Media's growth strategy team completed a 12-week market analysis for its streaming platform, covering subscriber behavior across 14 demographic segments. The strategy team has 22 members across two divisions. Leadership reviewed the analysis at a two-day off-site. The recommended content investment is projected to generate $50,000,000 in incremental annual revenue through improved subscriber acquisition and reduced churn.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the strategic alignment of Apex Digital's current content investment plan?

version_b.prompt_text:
Apex Digital Media's growth strategy team completed a 12-week market analysis for its streaming platform, covering subscriber behavior across 14 demographic segments. The strategy team has 22 members across two divisions. Leadership reviewed the analysis at a two-day off-site. Without the recommended content investment, the platform would forgo $50,000,000 in annual revenue currently being captured by competing services.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the strategic alignment of Apex Digital's current content investment plan?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the content investment as generating $50M in incremental revenue (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as losing $50M in revenue to competitors (loss). The question asks about strategic alignment.

control.prompt_text: A compliance officer compares two audit strategies. Strategy 1 catches 60 violations per cycle with certainty. Strategy 2 catches 200 violations per cycle with 25% probability and 0 with 75% probability. Which strategy catches more violations in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Strategy 1 — 60 violations caught for certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Strategy 2 — 50 violations caught in expectation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Strategy 1 catches more violations in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both strategies catch the same number in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: Sophia, a sales manager, says: 'I'd rather offer a guaranteed $20 discount than enter a lottery that gives customers a 25% chance of a $100 discount, even though the lottery costs the company less in expectation.' Is Sophia's preference rational from the firm's cost-minimization perspective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — guaranteed discounts build more customer loyalty

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — from a cost perspective the lottery (EV $25 per customer) costs more than the guaranteed $20 discount, so Sophia should prefer the guaranteed discount for different reasons than she states

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes — customers prefer certainty, making the guaranteed discount more effective

choices.label: D
choices.text: The firm should test both options with a pilot study

version_a.prompt_text:
Summit Cloud Services' platform engineering team rolled out a new deployment pipeline with automated rollback capabilities nine months ago. The site-reliability team monitored 2,400 deployments across four production environments. The platform engineering group has 31 engineers organized in three squads. The new pipeline has reduced deployment-related incidents by 35%.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of technical leadership in Summit Cloud's platform engineering team?

version_b.prompt_text:
Summit Cloud Services' platform engineering team rolled out a new deployment pipeline with automated rollback capabilities nine months ago. The site-reliability team monitored 2,400 deployments across four production environments. The platform engineering group has 31 engineers organized in three squads. Without the new pipeline, the team would experience 35% more deployment-related incidents than it does today.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of technical leadership in Summit Cloud's platform engineering team?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the pipeline as achieving a 35% incident reduction (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 35% higher incident rate (loss). The question asks about technical leadership quality.

control.prompt_text: Two marketing campaigns are proposed. Campaign Alpha costs $300,000 and is guaranteed to acquire 1,500 new customers. Campaign Beta costs $300,000 and acquires 5,000 new customers with 20% probability and 500 customers with 80% probability. Which campaign acquires more customers in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Campaign Alpha — 1,500 customers certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Campaign Beta — expected 1,400 customers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Campaign Alpha acquires more customers in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both campaigns acquire the same customers in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A city planner argues: 'We must invest $30,000,000 now to prevent a bridge replacement that has only a 15% probability of being needed in the next decade, at a replacement cost of $80,000,000.' Evaluate this solely on expected-value grounds.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Invest — infrastructure risk is never worth ignoring

choices.label: B
choices.text: Do not invest — the expected replacement cost ($12,000,000) is less than the immediate investment ($30,000,000), making investment negative-EV

choices.label: C
choices.text: Invest — the catastrophic scenario justifies precautionary spending

choices.label: D
choices.text: Commission a structural engineering study before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Ironwood Consulting Group's enterprise sales division completed its fiscal-year pipeline review. The division covers eight industry verticals and manages relationships with 230 active accounts. The sales enablement team updated training materials and CRM workflows in the second quarter. The new proposal-to-close process has added $3,200,000 in annual recurring contract value through improved win rates on competitive deals.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the current performance level of Ironwood's enterprise sales team?

version_b.prompt_text:
Ironwood Consulting Group's enterprise sales division completed its fiscal-year pipeline review. The division covers eight industry verticals and manages relationships with 230 active accounts. The sales enablement team updated training materials and CRM workflows in the second quarter. Without the new proposal-to-close process, the division would be losing $3,200,000 in annual recurring contract value to competitors on contested deals.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the current performance level of Ironwood's enterprise sales team?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the process improvement as adding $3.2M in contract value (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding $3.2M in competitive losses (loss). The question asks about sales team performance.

control.prompt_text: A renewable energy project can pursue two subsidy routes. Route 1 secures $5,000,000 in grants with certainty. Route 2 secures $12,000,000 with 50% probability and $0 with 50% probability. Which route yields higher expected funding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Route 1 — $5,000,000 certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Route 2 — expected $6,000,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both routes yield the same expected funding

choices.label: D
choices.text: Route 1 yields higher expected funding

explicit.prompt_text: A technology lead says: 'I won't migrate to a more efficient cloud platform because a migration gone wrong could cost us two weeks of downtime — even though the probability is 10% and the expected cost is far less than the annual savings.' What is the rational recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid migration — operational continuity must be protected above all

choices.label: B
choices.text: Proceed with migration — the tech lead is overweighting the loss scenario relative to the larger expected savings, a loss-aversion error

choices.label: C
choices.text: Wait until a better migration tool is available

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negotiate an SLA with the cloud provider before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Kelvin Biotech's talent acquisition team redesigned its technical hiring process following a six-month audit of candidate drop-off rates. The team spans 14 recruiters serving five business divisions. The redesign involved structured interview calibration and offer-stage SLA tracking. The new process has reduced average time-to-fill for engineering roles by 25%.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the health of Kelvin Biotech's technical talent pipeline?

version_b.prompt_text:
Kelvin Biotech's talent acquisition team redesigned its technical hiring process following a six-month audit of candidate drop-off rates. The team spans 14 recruiters serving five business divisions. The redesign involved structured interview calibration and offer-stage SLA tracking. Without the new process, average time-to-fill for engineering roles would remain 25% higher than the current benchmark.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the health of Kelvin Biotech's technical talent pipeline?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the hiring redesign as achieving a 25% time-to-fill reduction (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 25% longer time-to-fill (loss). The question asks about talent pipeline health.

control.prompt_text: A warehouse manager must choose between two inventory replenishment policies. Policy M avoids 200 stockout events per year with certainty. Policy N avoids all 500 stockout events per year with 35% probability and avoids none with 65% probability. Which policy avoids more stockouts in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Policy M — 200 stockouts avoided with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Policy N — expected 175 stockouts avoided

choices.label: C
choices.text: Policy M avoids more stockouts in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both policies are equivalent in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: An analyst presents two contract options to a committee. Contract A guarantees $800,000 revenue. Contract B offers $2,000,000 with 60% probability and $100,000 with 40% probability. The committee chair says: 'I can't stomach the risk of the lower outcome on Contract B.' What does EV analysis say?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The chair is right — the guaranteed revenue provides essential cash-flow certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contract B has higher EV ($1,240,000 vs $800,000); the chair's risk aversion, while understandable, conflicts with expected-value maximization

choices.label: C
choices.text: The committee should renegotiate Contract B to reduce the downside

choices.label: D
choices.text: Choose Contract A — certainty is always preferable for institutional decision-making

version_a.prompt_text:
Meridian Capital Partners restructured its legal entity framework across seven jurisdictions following a three-year tax optimization review. The tax advisory team worked alongside external counsel and covered 22 operating subsidiaries. The restructuring was completed in the fourth quarter. The new structure generates $18,000,000 in annual tax savings through optimized transfer pricing and entity elections.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of executive decision-making reflected in this restructuring initiative?

version_b.prompt_text:
Meridian Capital Partners restructured its legal entity framework across seven jurisdictions following a three-year tax optimization review. The tax advisory team worked alongside external counsel and covered 22 operating subsidiaries. The restructuring was completed in the fourth quarter. Without the new structure, the firm would continue losing $18,000,000 annually through suboptimal transfer pricing and entity elections.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of executive decision-making reflected in this restructuring initiative?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the restructuring as generating $18M in annual tax savings (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding an $18M annual tax loss (loss). The question asks about executive decision-making quality.

control.prompt_text: Two software patches address the same vulnerability. Patch A eliminates the vulnerability entirely at a cost of $40,000. Patch B costs $10,000 and reduces breach probability from 20% to 5%; a breach costs $300,000. Compared to doing nothing, which patch yields greater expected cost reduction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patch A — eliminates $60,000 expected loss at cost of $40,000; net benefit $20,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Patch B — reduces expected loss by $45,000 at cost of $10,000; net benefit $35,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both patches yield equal net expected benefit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patch A yields greater net expected benefit

explicit.prompt_text: Described as a 'loss': a market expansion that has a 40% chance of reducing market share by 5 percentage points. Described as a 'gain': a 60% chance of increasing market share by 3 percentage points. The leadership team says the gain version sounds far more appealing despite identical strategic substance. What does this demonstrate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gains are objectively more valuable than avoiding equivalent losses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain/loss framing bias — the leadership team's preference is shaped by language rather than underlying expected value, distorting rational evaluation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Leadership teams are right to prefer positive-framed initiatives for cultural reasons

choices.label: D
choices.text: The two framings describe different strategic realities, so preference differences are justified

version_a.prompt_text:
Sunridge Retail's supply-chain team renegotiated its primary vendor contracts following a market benchmarking exercise across nine product categories. The procurement team includes 19 buyers and analysts. The renegotiation process covered 34 vendor agreements and took four months. The new contracts have reduced supply-chain costs by 12% compared to the prior-year agreements.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the strength of Sunridge Retail's vendor relationships as reflected in these outcomes?

version_b.prompt_text:
Sunridge Retail's supply-chain team renegotiated its primary vendor contracts following a market benchmarking exercise across nine product categories. The procurement team includes 19 buyers and analysts. The renegotiation process covered 34 vendor agreements and took four months. Without the renegotiated contracts, the company would be paying 12% more in supply-chain costs than current market rates.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the strength of Sunridge Retail's vendor relationships as reflected in these outcomes?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the renegotiation as a 12% cost reduction (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a 12% cost premium (loss). The question asks about vendor relationship strength.

control.prompt_text: A pharmaceutical firm evaluates two drug candidates. Drug X has a 70% chance of Phase III success yielding $80,000,000 profit and a 30% chance of failure costing $10,000,000. Drug Y has a 50% chance of success yielding $120,000,000 and a 50% chance of failure costing $20,000,000. Which drug has higher expected profit?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both drugs have equal expected profit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Drug X has higher expected profit

explicit.prompt_text: A compliance officer says: 'We passed last year's audit, so I don't want to invest in new controls — any investment is a certain loss, but another audit failure is just a possibility.' The fine for failure is $500,000 and the probability is 20%. New controls cost $80,000. What does EV analysis advise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Skip the controls — last year's clean audit is strong evidence of continuing compliance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Invest in controls — the expected fine ($100,000) exceeds the control cost ($80,000); the officer is irrationally treating certain costs as worse than higher expected uncertain costs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Invest in controls only if the audit is happening soon

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seek a legal opinion on audit risk before investing

version_a.prompt_text:
Harborview Bank's legal and compliance group completed a review of its dispute resolution procedures for retail lending customers. The review covered 14 months of complaint data and involved the compliance, legal, and operations teams. The group has 42 staff across three practice areas. The new early-resolution protocol has reduced litigation exposure, enabling the bank to release $7,500,000 from its litigation reserve.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the overall governance health of Harborview Bank's legal and compliance function?

version_b.prompt_text:
Harborview Bank's legal and compliance group completed a review of its dispute resolution procedures for retail lending customers. The review covered 14 months of complaint data and involved the compliance, legal, and operations teams. The group has 42 staff across three practice areas. Without the new early-resolution protocol, the bank would need to maintain $7,500,000 in additional litigation reserve to cover elevated dispute exposure.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the overall governance health of Harborview Bank's legal and compliance function?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the protocol as releasing $7.5M from reserve (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as requiring $7.5M in additional reserve without the protocol (loss). The question asks about organizational governance health.

control.prompt_text: A city must decide between two flood-barrier designs. Design 1 costs $10,000,000 and reduces flood damage from $50,000,000 to $0 with certainty. Design 2 costs $4,000,000 and reduces flood probability from 40% to 10%. A flood causes $50,000,000 damage. Which design has the lower total expected cost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Design 1 — total expected cost $10,000,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Design 2 — total expected cost $4,000,000 + $5,000,000 = $9,000,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both designs have the same total expected cost

choices.label: D
choices.text: Design 1 has the lower total expected cost

explicit.prompt_text: A brand manager argues: 'Our product recall will destroy brand equity — I'd rather run a 30% chance of a $10,000,000 lawsuit than lock in $1,500,000 of certain recall costs.' Evaluate this from an expected-value standpoint.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The manager is correct — brand equity damage from a recall is incalculable

choices.label: B
choices.text: The manager is wrong on EV grounds: the expected lawsuit cost ($3,000,000) exceeds the certain recall cost ($1,500,000); avoiding the certain cost is loss-averse reasoning that increases expected expense

choices.label: C
choices.text: Commission consumer research before deciding on a recall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negotiate with plaintiffs before recalling

version_a.prompt_text:
Portsmith SaaS completed a cohort analysis of its mid-market customer base following the rollout of a proactive health-score monitoring system. The customer success team reviewed 1,800 accounts across five industry segments. The CS team has 24 managers and analysts. The health-score system has reduced annual customer churn by 28 percentage points within the monitored cohort.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Portsmith's customer success organization?

version_b.prompt_text:
Portsmith SaaS completed a cohort analysis of its mid-market customer base following the rollout of a proactive health-score monitoring system. The customer success team reviewed 1,800 accounts across five industry segments. The CS team has 24 managers and analysts. Without the health-score system, annual customer churn in the monitored cohort would be 28 percentage points higher than it currently is.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Portsmith's customer success organization?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the monitoring system as reducing churn by 28 points (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as churn being 28 points higher without the system (loss). The question asks about customer success effectiveness.

control.prompt_text: An insurance policy costs $1,500 per year. Without the policy, there is a 3% chance of a $40,000 loss. Purely on expected value, should a risk-neutral agent purchase the policy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — expected loss without policy ($1,200) is less than premium ($1,500), so do not buy

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — expected loss without policy ($1,200) is less than premium ($1,500)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes — the policy provides positive expected value regardless of premium

choices.label: D
choices.text: A risk-neutral agent is indifferent when comparing $1,200 vs $1,500

explicit.prompt_text: An HR director says: 'I won't eliminate the underused employee lounge because staff will perceive it as a loss, even though converting the space would save $120,000 annually and surveys show 80% of staff never use it.' What is the rational recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep the lounge — employee perception is a legitimate business concern

choices.label: B
choices.text: Convert the space — the director is letting anticipated loss aversion in others distort a straightforward cost-savings decision

choices.label: C
choices.text: Survey staff again before making a final decision

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phase out the lounge gradually to reduce perceived loss

version_a.prompt_text:
Halcyon Systems' platform team completed a two-year software rationalization program across its internal developer toolchain. The initiative involved consolidating 37 licensed tools into a unified platform. The engineering organization has 94 developers and architects across six product areas. The rationalization program has saved $1,100,000 in annual software licensing fees through vendor consolidation and contract renegotiation.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of Halcyon Systems' technical debt management practices?

version_b.prompt_text:
Halcyon Systems' platform team completed a two-year software rationalization program across its internal developer toolchain. The initiative involved consolidating 37 licensed tools into a unified platform. The engineering organization has 94 developers and architects across six product areas. Without the rationalization program, the company would be losing $1,100,000 annually to redundant software licensing fees.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of Halcyon Systems' technical debt management practices?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the rationalization as saving $1.1M annually (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as avoiding a $1.1M annual licensing loss (loss). The question asks about technical debt management quality.

control.prompt_text: Option A: a certain gain of $1,000. Option B: an 80% chance of gaining $1,600 and a 20% chance of gaining $0. Which option has the higher expected value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Option A — EV = $1,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Option B — EV = $1,280

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both options have equal expected value

choices.label: D
choices.text: Option A has higher expected value

explicit.prompt_text: A supply-chain manager refuses to switch suppliers even though the new supplier has 20% lower costs and a 95% reliability rating. The manager says: 'What if the new supplier fails? Switching is a gamble we can't afford to lose.' What does rational analysis recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stay with the current supplier — known reliability trumps cost savings

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switch — the manager is overweighting the small probability of supplier failure relative to the large, certain cost savings; this is loss-aversion bias

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dual-source from both suppliers for a transition period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negotiate a reliability guarantee with the new supplier before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Clearwater Health Systems implemented a redesigned employee wellness program across its network of six regional hospitals. The HR analytics team tracked unplanned absenteeism rates over 24 months, covering 3,400 clinical and administrative staff. The wellness program has reduced unplanned absenteeism by 9 percentage points compared to the prior two-year baseline.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the health and engagement level of Clearwater's workforce under this program?

version_b.prompt_text:
Clearwater Health Systems implemented a redesigned employee wellness program across its network of six regional hospitals. The HR analytics team tracked unplanned absenteeism rates over 24 months, covering 3,400 clinical and administrative staff. Without the wellness program, unplanned absenteeism across the network would be 9 percentage points higher than current levels.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the health and engagement level of Clearwater's workforce under this program?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the wellness program as reducing absenteeism by 9 points (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as a 9-point absenteeism increase without the program (loss). The question asks about workforce health and engagement.

control.prompt_text: A retailer is comparing two restocking approaches. Approach 1 fills exactly 400 orders per day with certainty. Approach 2 fills 1,000 orders per day with 35% probability and 100 orders per day with 65% probability. Which approach fills more orders in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Approach 1 — 400 orders certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Approach 2 — expected 415 orders per day

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both approaches fill the same number of orders in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Approach 1 fills more orders in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A portfolio manager says: 'I know the math says to sell this asset at a 10% loss and reinvest, but the psychological hit of recording that loss is stopping me.' What is the normatively correct action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hold — psychological comfort is a legitimate component of decision utility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sell and reinvest — recorded losses are sunk costs; decisions should be based on prospective expected value, not on the pain of acknowledging past losses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hold until the asset recovers to the purchase price

choices.label: D
choices.text: Consult a behavioral finance advisor before deciding

version_a.prompt_text:
Terracrest Building Materials' finance team deployed an AI-assisted credit-risk screening tool for new commercial accounts. The implementation covered 1,600 active customer accounts across four sales regions. The finance operations team has 28 staff managing credit, collections, and reporting. The screening tool has reduced annual accounts-receivable write-offs by $425,000 through earlier identification of at-risk accounts.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Terracrest's financial controls?

version_b.prompt_text:
Terracrest Building Materials' finance team deployed an AI-assisted credit-risk screening tool for new commercial accounts. The implementation covered 1,600 active customer accounts across four sales regions. The finance operations team has 28 staff managing credit, collections, and reporting. Without the screening tool, the company would continue losing $425,000 annually to preventable accounts-receivable write-offs.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Terracrest's financial controls?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the screening tool as reducing write-offs by $425K (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as ongoing $425K annual write-off losses without the tool (loss). The question asks about financial controls effectiveness.

control.prompt_text: A venture capital firm is assessing two startups. Startup P has a 60% chance of returning $5,000,000 and a 40% chance of returning $500,000. Startup Q has a 90% chance of returning $3,000,000 and a 10% chance of returning $300,000. Which startup has the higher expected return?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Startup P — EV = $3,200,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Startup Q — EV = $2,730,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both startups have equal expected returns

choices.label: D
choices.text: Startup P has the higher expected return

explicit.prompt_text: A department head rejects a proposed reorganization that yields a net efficiency gain of 18%, saying: 'The transition period will feel like a step backward, and I don't want my team to experience that decline.' What does this illustrate about the department head's decision-making?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Responsible leadership — protecting team morale during transitions is essential

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss aversion is causing the department head to reject a net-positive change by overweighting the temporary transitional dip relative to the lasting efficiency gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: The department head needs a better change-management plan before proceeding

choices.label: D
choices.text: An 18% efficiency gain may not justify the disruption costs

version_a.prompt_text:
Bluestone Commerce upgraded its order management system across all three fulfillment centers last quarter. The operations team processed a benchmarking study comparing throughput before and after the upgrade across 48,000 orders. The fulfillment network employs 580 warehouse staff. The upgraded system has reduced order processing time per unit by 20%.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate Bluestone Commerce's operational readiness for handling increased order volume?

version_b.prompt_text:
Bluestone Commerce upgraded its order management system across all three fulfillment centers last quarter. The operations team processed a benchmarking study comparing throughput before and after the upgrade across 48,000 orders. The fulfillment network employs 580 warehouse staff. Without the upgraded system, order processing time per unit would be 20% slower than current levels.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate Bluestone Commerce's operational readiness for handling increased order volume?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the system upgrade as a 20% processing time reduction (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as being 20% slower without the upgrade (loss). The question asks about operational readiness for scaling.

control.prompt_text: A supply-chain analyst evaluates two sourcing deals. Deal 1 saves $120,000 in procurement costs with certainty. Deal 2 saves $300,000 with 45% probability and saves $0 with 55% probability. Which deal saves more in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deal 1 — $120,000 saved with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deal 2 — expected savings of $135,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both deals save the same amount in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deal 1 saves more in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: An executive says: 'If we present the new compensation plan as eliminating a pay cut employees already experienced, they'll react more positively than if we present it as a pay raise of equivalent size.' What bias is the executive exploiting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anchoring bias — employees are anchored to their previous salary

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain/loss framing — restoring a loss feels more valuable than receiving an equivalent gain; the executive is leveraging asymmetric loss sensitivity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Representativeness bias — employees judge fairness by category

choices.label: D
choices.text: Availability heuristic — the pay cut is more memorable than a hypothetical gain

version_a.prompt_text:
Vanguard Industrial Solutions' pricing committee rolled out a new discount approval workflow for its field sales team. The workflow applies to deals above $50,000 across all six product lines. The sales operations team has 17 staff covering quoting, pricing, and analytics. The new workflow has saved $680,000 in unnecessary discounting over the past three quarters without measurable impact on win rates.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the pricing discipline and margin health of Vanguard's sales organization?

version_b.prompt_text:
Vanguard Industrial Solutions' pricing committee rolled out a new discount approval workflow for its field sales team. The workflow applies to deals above $50,000 across all six product lines. The sales operations team has 17 staff covering quoting, pricing, and analytics. Without the new workflow, the organization would continue losing $680,000 per three-quarter period to unnecessary discounting, with no corresponding gain in win rates.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the pricing discipline and margin health of Vanguard's sales organization?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the workflow as saving $680K in discounting (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as losing $680K to unnecessary discounting without the workflow (loss). The question asks about pricing discipline and margin health.

control.prompt_text: A hospital administrator must choose between two infection-control protocols. Protocol A prevents 50 infections per quarter with certainty. Protocol B prevents 180 infections per quarter with 25% probability and 0 infections with 75% probability. Which protocol prevents more infections in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protocol A — 50 infections prevented for certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Protocol B — expected 45 infections prevented

choices.label: C
choices.text: Protocol A prevents more infections in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both protocols prevent the same number in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A logistics team prefers paying $50,000 for insurance that eliminates a 20% chance of a $200,000 freight loss, rather than self-insuring. Expected loss without insurance: $40,000. Is this decision rational?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — the peace of mind from insurance has real value

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — from a pure EV standpoint the insurance premium ($50,000) exceeds the expected loss ($40,000), so the team is paying extra due to loss aversion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes — freight disruptions carry secondary operational costs beyond the direct financial loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depends on the firm's cash-flow situation

version_a.prompt_text:
Westfield Energy's ethics and compliance team launched a refreshed training curriculum for its 2,100-person workforce following a third-party program audit. The curriculum covers anti-corruption, data handling, and conflict-of-interest policies. The compliance team has 19 staff. The refreshed curriculum has reduced self-reported policy violations by 45% over two compliance cycles.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of Westfield Energy's compliance training program?

version_b.prompt_text:
Westfield Energy's ethics and compliance team launched a refreshed training curriculum for its 2,100-person workforce following a third-party program audit. The curriculum covers anti-corruption, data handling, and conflict-of-interest policies. The compliance team has 19 staff. Without the refreshed curriculum, self-reported policy violations would be 45% higher than current levels.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the quality of Westfield Energy's compliance training program?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the curriculum as reducing violations by 45% (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as violations being 45% higher without the curriculum (loss). The question asks about compliance training program quality.

control.prompt_text: Two process-improvement programs are under review. Program X is certain to reduce unit production time by 12 minutes. Program Y reduces unit production time by 30 minutes with 30% probability and by 6 minutes with 70% probability. Which program reduces production time more in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Program X — certain 12-minute reduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Program Y — expected 13.2-minute reduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both programs reduce production time equally in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Program X reduces time more in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A research director declines a collaboration that has an 85% chance of producing a breakthrough (value: $5,000,000) and a 15% chance of producing nothing useful (cost: $200,000 sunk). She says, 'I can't risk that 15% failure.' What is the rational recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decline — reputational risk from failure justifies caution

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accept — the EV is highly positive ($4,220,000 net); refusing due to a 15% failure probability is loss-averse irrationality

choices.label: C
choices.text: Negotiate to reduce the 15% failure probability before agreeing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Seek additional partnerships to hedge the collaboration risk

version_a.prompt_text:
Arcturus Software adopted a continuous-discovery framework across its four core product lines 18 months ago. The product and design teams conduct weekly customer interviews with 30-minute structured sessions. The product organization has 55 staff including product managers, designers, and researchers. The framework has saved $6,200,000 in development costs by identifying low-value feature work before it enters engineering sprints.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the maturity of Arcturus Software's product development process?

version_b.prompt_text:
Arcturus Software adopted a continuous-discovery framework across its four core product lines 18 months ago. The product and design teams conduct weekly customer interviews with 30-minute structured sessions. The product organization has 55 staff including product managers, designers, and researchers. Without the framework, the company would be wasting $6,200,000 in development costs on low-value feature work that never reaches production-ready quality.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the maturity of Arcturus Software's product development process?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the framework as saving $6.2M in development costs (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as wasting $6.2M without the framework (loss). The question asks about product development process maturity.

control.prompt_text: An energy auditor compares two retrofit packages. Package A saves $25,000 in annual energy costs with certainty. Package B saves $70,000 with 40% probability and $5,000 with 60% probability. Which package saves more energy cost in expectation per year?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Package A — $25,000 saved with certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Package B — expected $31,000 saved per year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both packages save the same amount in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Package A saves more in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A logistics VP says: 'Switching to a new routing algorithm is risky — if it underperforms, costs go up. Staying put keeps costs stable.' The algorithm has a 70% chance of saving $400,000 annually and a 30% chance of costing an extra $50,000. What does EV analysis recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stay put — operational stability is worth more than expected savings

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switch — EV of switching is +$265,000; the VP is overweighting the 30% downside

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pilot the algorithm in one region before committing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evaluate after one more quarter of current-system data

version_a.prompt_text:
Ferncroft Technologies' SRE team completed a 10-month alert rationalization project across its production microservices environment. The team reviewed alert coverage for 180 services and eliminated duplicate and low-signal notifications. The SRE team has 16 engineers across two time-zone shifts. The rationalization project has reduced actionable on-call pages by 50%, allowing engineers to focus on preventive work.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Ferncroft's site reliability engineering function?

version_b.prompt_text:
Ferncroft Technologies' SRE team completed a 10-month alert rationalization project across its production microservices environment. The team reviewed alert coverage for 180 services and eliminated duplicate and low-signal notifications. The SRE team has 16 engineers across two time-zone shifts. Without the rationalization project, engineers would be handling 50% more on-call pages than they currently receive, leaving less capacity for preventive work.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Ferncroft's site reliability engineering function?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the alert rationalization as a 50% reduction in pages (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as enduring 50% more pages without the project (loss). The question asks about SRE effectiveness.

control.prompt_text: A product manager compares two launch strategies. Launch A generates $500,000 in first-year revenue with certainty. Launch B generates $1,200,000 with 50% probability and $50,000 with 50% probability. Which launch generates more expected first-year revenue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Launch A — $500,000 certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Launch B — expected $625,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both launches generate the same expected revenue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Launch A generates more expected revenue

explicit.prompt_text: A product team frames the same security feature two ways: (A) 'This update protects 90% of users from data exposure' and (B) 'Without this update, 10% of users remain vulnerable.' The team finds framing B generates far more urgency. What does this reflect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Effective risk communication — loss frames are legitimately more motivating

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain/loss framing bias — the same probability (10% exposed) produces different urgency based purely on whether it is framed as a remaining loss vs. an achieved gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Availability heuristic — concrete descriptions of vulnerable users feel more real

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both framings describe different realities and are not equivalent

version_a.prompt_text:
Stonebridge Capital's HR operations team built an in-house sourcing capability to replace its reliance on external recruiting agencies for analyst and associate roles. The initiative took eight months and included training three internal sourcers. The HR team covers 520 employees across three business lines. The in-house program has generated $310,000 in annual savings by replacing external agency placements for 18 roles per year.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the cost management effectiveness of Stonebridge Capital's HR operations?

version_b.prompt_text:
Stonebridge Capital's HR operations team built an in-house sourcing capability to replace its reliance on external recruiting agencies for analyst and associate roles. The initiative took eight months and included training three internal sourcers. The HR team covers 520 employees across three business lines. Without the in-house program, the company would lose $310,000 annually to external agency fees for roles that could be filled internally.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the cost management effectiveness of Stonebridge Capital's HR operations?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the in-house program as saving $310K annually (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as losing $310K annually to agency fees without the program (loss). The question asks about HR operational cost management.

control.prompt_text: A financial analyst compares two bond portfolios. Portfolio 1 returns 5% annually with certainty on a $2,000,000 position. Portfolio 2 returns 12% with 40% probability and 1% with 60% probability on the same position. Which portfolio has the higher expected annual return?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Portfolio 1 — certain 5% return ($100,000)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Portfolio 2 — expected 5.4% return ($108,000)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both portfolios have the same expected return

choices.label: D
choices.text: Portfolio 1 has a higher expected return

explicit.prompt_text: A board member says: 'I know we should diversify the supply chain, but every dollar spent on diversification is a certain cost I can point to. The supply disruption it prevents is just a probability.' The diversification costs $500,000 and prevents expected disruption losses of $900,000. Is the board member's hesitation rational?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes — certain costs are fundamentally different from probabilistic ones in budget planning

choices.label: B
choices.text: No — the certain cost ($500,000) is less than the expected benefit ($900,000); the hesitation reflects loss aversion in the form of preferring to avoid certain costs over expected savings

choices.label: C
choices.text: The board member should request a third-party risk assessment first

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diversification is advisable only when disruption probability exceeds 50%

version_a.prompt_text:
Dalton Manufacturing's accounts receivable team implemented an automated invoice-follow-up system for its 420 active trade accounts. The system was piloted for six months before full deployment across the finance department. The AR team has 11 analysts managing collections and dispute resolution. The automated system has reduced average collection days by 17%, improving working capital turnover across the business.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the financial process efficiency of Dalton Manufacturing's AR function?

version_b.prompt_text:
Dalton Manufacturing's accounts receivable team implemented an automated invoice-follow-up system for its 420 active trade accounts. The system was piloted for six months before full deployment across the finance department. The AR team has 11 analysts managing collections and dispute resolution. Without the automated system, average collection days would remain 17% longer than the current benchmark, constraining working capital turnover.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the financial process efficiency of Dalton Manufacturing's AR function?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the AR system as reducing collection days by 17% (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as collection days being 17% longer without the system (loss). The question asks about financial process efficiency.

control.prompt_text: A data center is evaluating two uptime-improvement plans. Plan Alpha reduces downtime by 200 hours per year with certainty. Plan Beta reduces downtime by 600 hours with 25% probability and by 100 hours with 75% probability. Which plan reduces more downtime in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Plan Alpha — 200 hours certain

choices.label: B
choices.text: Plan Beta — expected 225 hours reduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both plans reduce the same downtime in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plan Alpha reduces more downtime in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: A finance team is evaluating whether to write off $3,000,000 in uncollectable receivables. The CFO says: 'Once we write it off, the loss is real. As long as it's on the books, it still feels like an asset.' What is the normatively correct action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delay write-off — keeping the asset on the books preserves optionality

choices.label: B
choices.text: Write off the receivables — their economic value is already zero; refusing to write them off is a denial of sunk costs driven by loss aversion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Engage a collections agency first to recover a portion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transfer the receivables to a subsidiary to avoid recording the loss

version_a.prompt_text:
Horizon Packaging redesigned its production scheduling process using lean manufacturing principles across three facilities. The operations excellence team conducted a six-month value-stream mapping exercise covering 22 production lines. The operations team has 240 production and logistics staff. The lean redesign has eliminated $4,800,000 in annual material waste and rework costs through tighter scheduling and reduced batch sizes.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the lean management culture at Horizon Packaging?

version_b.prompt_text:
Horizon Packaging redesigned its production scheduling process using lean manufacturing principles across three facilities. The operations excellence team conducted a six-month value-stream mapping exercise covering 22 production lines. The operations team has 240 production and logistics staff. Without the lean redesign, the company would continue losing $4,800,000 annually to material waste and rework caused by loose scheduling and oversized batch runs.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the lean management culture at Horizon Packaging?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the lean redesign as eliminating $4.8M in annual waste (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as ongoing $4.8M annual waste without the redesign (loss). The question asks about lean management culture.

control.prompt_text: A talent acquisition team compares two hiring pipelines. Pipeline 1 fills open roles in an average of 30 days with certainty. Pipeline 2 fills roles in 15 days with 50% probability and 55 days with 50% probability. Which pipeline fills roles faster in expectation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pipeline 1 — certain 30-day fill time

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pipeline 2 — expected 35-day fill time

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both pipelines fill roles at the same speed in expectation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pipeline 1 fills roles faster in expectation

explicit.prompt_text: An engineering team resists adopting automated testing because 'every bug the automation misses will feel like a failure, even if we catch far more bugs overall.' The automation reduces total defects by 60% compared to manual testing. What does rational analysis recommend?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resist adoption — individual visible failures damage team credibility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adopt automation — the team is letting loss aversion about individually visible failures override a 60% net reduction in total defects, a clearly superior outcome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Run automation in parallel with manual testing indefinitely

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pilot automation on low-stakes modules first

version_a.prompt_text:
Osprey Retail's customer experience team introduced a real-time feedback loop linking post-purchase surveys directly to store-level coaching workflows. The program was deployed across 92 retail locations over a nine-month rollout. The CX team has 26 staff managing surveys, analytics, and frontline coaching. The program has improved average Net Promoter Score by 11 points across participating locations.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Osprey Retail's customer experience program?

version_b.prompt_text:
Osprey Retail's customer experience team introduced a real-time feedback loop linking post-purchase surveys directly to store-level coaching workflows. The program was deployed across 92 retail locations over a nine-month rollout. The CX team has 26 staff managing surveys, analytics, and frontline coaching. Without the program, average Net Promoter Score across participating locations would be 11 points lower than current levels.

On a scale from 1 (very low) to 10 (very high), how would you rate the effectiveness of Osprey Retail's customer experience program?

implicit.manipulation_description: version_a frames the CX program as achieving an 11-point NPS improvement (gain); version_b frames the same outcome as NPS being 11 points lower without the program (loss). The question asks about customer experience program effectiveness.
